title: Bold Modern L-Shape Rotating Desk
summary: A two-piece L-shaped workstation: a bookshelf tabletop and a desk tabletop, matched in height and joined only at a shared felt-padded corner, so the desk portion can pivot away from the bookshelf.
description:
This is a matched two-piece system rather than one fixed L-shaped desk — a long bookshelf unit and a separate desk unit, both built the exact same height and only meeting at one shared corner cushioned with felt pads. **Height matching between the two pieces is the single hard requirement of this whole build** — measure the bookshelf's overall height once it's assembled and cut the desk legs to match exactly, rather than trusting the plan's stated numbers, since real plywood thickness and assembly tolerances shift the final height slightly.

Both tabletops share the same construction method: 2x4s ripped down to 3" wide form the perimeter frame, with 3/4" plywood panels set inside and attached with pocket holes carefully placed so they land hidden under the shelves once everything is assembled. **The center plywood panel on the bookshelf needs to be perfectly centered** on the tabletop frame — this is what keeps the finished shelving symmetrical rather than visibly off to one side.

Build the bookshelf tabletop completely first — legs, panels, bottom trim, shelves, and shelf trim — before repeating the same steps for the desk tabletop. The desk's own inside leg gets plywood panels on both faces, flush to the outside and cleated on the inside, which is what lets the desk pivot against the bookshelf corner on felt pads rather than being screwed rigidly to it — an ADA-minded detail that keeps the whole workstation reconfigurable rather than fixed in one L shape permanently.

materials.name: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Rip the stock, then cut the parts
steps.body: Rip first, cut second: put both plywood sheets through the table saw as 15-3/4" strips and rip every 2x4 down to 3" wide in one fence setting, since all the frame and leg parts share that width. From the plywood cut the bookshelf top at 92", two bookshelf sides at 24", the bookshelf center at 26-1/4", the desk top at 67", the desk outer leg panel at 27" and the inside leg panel at 29-1/4"; leave the four shelves long for now and cut them to the bays you actually get. From the ripped 2x4 cut four tabletop end frames at 15-3/4", six bookshelf legs at 24", two desk legs at 27", and the side frames at 95" for the bookshelf and 70" for the desk. Hold the desk leg and outer panel lengths until the bookshelf is standing — they are cut to its measured height, not to the numbers here, and matching the two heights is the one hard requirement of this build.
materials: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets

steps.title: Build the bookshelf tabletop frame
steps.body: Build the tabletop as a frame around the 92" plywood panel: the two 15-3/4" end frames across the ends and the two 95" side frames along the long edges, all set on edge so the panel sits inside a 3"-tall lip. The 92" panel plus a 1-1/2" end frame at each end is exactly the 95" side frame length — that is the check that these parts belong together, so dry-fit and confirm it before drilling. Join the corners with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, screw the panel to the frame from underneath, and compare the frame's diagonals before the last screws go in.
materials: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets

steps.title: Attach the bookshelf legs and panels
steps.body: Turn the tabletop upside down and fasten the six 24" legs to the underside of the frame with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws — a pair at each end and a pair at the middle, all plumb to the frame. Set the two 24" side panels and the 26-1/4" center panel in and screw them to the frame, holding every panel tight up under the tabletop; the plan does not explain why the center panel runs 2-1/4" longer than the sides, so register them all at the top and check the case stands level on all six legs before you trust it. Center the center panel by measuring from each end to the same face of the panel rather than by eye — the whole shelving grid is symmetric about it. Place each panel's pocket holes low enough that a shelf will later cover them.
materials: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets

steps.title: Add the bookshelf bottom supports and shelves
steps.body: Run the four 1x3 bottom supports along the front and back of each bay at the bottom, screwed into the side and center panels, so the lowest shelf sits on a ledge instead of hanging on screws alone. Then measure each bay and cut the shelves to it — the cut list's 46-3/8" is longer than half of the 92" case allows once the 3/4" center panel is counted, so trim to your actual openings rather than to that number. Set two shelves in each bay, fasten with pocket hole screws into the panels, and level each one before driving the second screw; a shelf this long shows its own slope from across the room.
materials: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets

steps.title: Trim the bookshelf shelves
steps.body: Face the front and back edge of each shelf with a 1x2 — eight pieces for four shelves — glued and pinned with 1-1/4" brad nails, which hides the plywood edge and stiffens the shelf against sagging. Cut each piece off the shelf it goes on rather than to the listed 43": that row is marked cut-to-fit and does not match the shelf length. Hold the trim flush with the shelf's top face so nothing catches a book being pushed in.

steps.title: Build the desk tabletop and legs
steps.body: Measure the assembled bookshelf's overall height at its top surface, and cut the two desk legs and the 27" outer leg panel to bring the desk to exactly that height — do it from the real piece, not from the plan's numbers, because plywood thickness and assembly tolerance move it. Then build the desk tabletop the same way as the bookshelf's: 15-3/4" end frames and 70" side frames around the 67" panel, corners pocket-screwed, panel screwed from below, diagonals checked. Once the desk stands, lay a straightedge across both tops — they have to read as one continuous surface where they meet.
materials: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets

steps.title: Build the desk inside leg
steps.body: The desk's inside leg gets a panel on both faces, which is what gives it a flat finished surface to meet the bookshelf. Glue and nail the outer plywood panel to the outside of the leg with 1-1/4" brads, flush with the leg's outer face. Then nail 1x2 cleats to the inside of the leg to stand the second panel off it — there is no cut-list row for these, so cut them from the spare 1x2 to the leg's height — and glue and nail the 29-1/4" inside panel onto the cleats. Add a 27" 1x2 leg trim to each exposed leg edge, and check the finished face is flat with a straightedge; this is the surface the desk will pivot against.
materials: 3/4" cabinet-grade plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheets

steps.title: Finish and join the two pieces
steps.body: Sand everything and apply primer and paint, or stain and poly, before the two pieces are put together — the mating faces at the corner are far easier to finish apart than in place. Stick the 1" felt pads under the bookshelf feet, under the desk feet, and on the desk face where it bears against the bookshelf corner; the pads are what let the desk pivot away without marking either piece, which is the whole reason nothing is screwed across that corner. Push the desk into position and lay a straightedge across both tops: if they aren't level with each other, that is the height error the build was cut to avoid, and it gets fixed at the desk legs.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: L-shaped desk paired with a matching bookshelf at a shared corner
